Summary: KLF4 functions as a tumor suppressor in human colorectal cancer. KLF4 is downregulated in CRC tissues compared with normal mucosa. KLF4 inhibits colorectal cancer growth in mice. KLF4 inhibits CRC cell proliferation by upregulating p21 and NDRG2 and by downregulating cyclinD1, GINS4, and IFITM3. KLF4 deficiency accelerates colitis and colon cancer and is associated with tumor development via epithelial–mesenchymal transition. MicroRNA‑92a and microRNA‑10b downregulate KLF4 and thereby promote colorectal cancer growth, migration, proliferation, and metastasis. The literature reports both that the transcription factor CDX2 negatively regulates KLF4 and that in colon cells CDX2 upregulates KLF4 by binding the KLF4 promoter and reducing association of H3K4 trimethylation with the KDM5B demethylase. In a clinical trial metformin increased KLF4 expression and positively affected colorectal cancer. APTO‑253, a KLF4 inducer, halted some advanced tumors in a trial but development stopped after reported MYC inhibition. Because transcription factors are considered undruggable, it is premature to treat KLF4 as a general cancer therapy target.
References: He, Z., He, J., & Xie, K. (2023). KLF4 transcription factor in tumorigenesis. Cell Death Discov, 9(1), 118. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41420-023-01416-y
